title: Obituary search job request (Matcher → Finder)
description: The POST /jobs request body. Carries PII-minimal, STRUCTURED person facts so each finder builds portal-optimal queries itself; pre-baked query strings are an optional hint only. Producer obligation: candidates MUST be people the matcher has reason to believe are DECEASED (apply the webtrees privacy filter first); never submit a living person.

schemaVersion.description: Major contract version. A finder MUST reject an unknown major.

jobId.description: Opaque, matcher-assigned job identifier. Used as a path segment in GET /jobs/{jobId}, hence the URL-safe charset. SHOULD be content-derived (a salted hash of the candidate set) so a collision implies identical content; see the 409 semantics.

locale.description: UI/search locale hint forwarded from webtrees: an ISO-639 language with an optional script subtag and/or region (e.g. "de", "de-DE", "zh-Hans", "sr-Latn"). A bounded BCP-47 subset covering webtrees' locale identifiers, not full BCP-47.

candidates.description: The people to search for. The finder treats every candidate independently. Bounded per job so a single request stays a manageable unit of work; the matcher splits larger sweeps across multiple jobs.

JobOptions.description: Optional, non-authoritative job-level hints. A finder MAY ignore any of these.

portals.description: Restrict the search to these portal ids (see capabilities). Absent = all portals the finder offers.

CandidateFacts.description: PII-minimal facts needed to SEARCH for one person. Deliberately excludes relatives — those are needed only for SCORING, which happens in the matcher, never in the finder. The matcher SHOULD omit residence places for any candidate whose death is not established.

personId.description: Opaque matcher reference. The finder echoes it back unchanged as the result key; it carries no meaning for the finder. URL/path-safe charset (same discipline as jobId) because the matcher may use it to derive a storage key.

names.description: One or more names to search under, including maiden (birth) and married names.

places.description: Known places (birth, baptism, residences …). Each value is the full, possibly comma-separated GEDCOM place hierarchy; the finder is expected to consider EVERY segment (Ortsteil) when matching, not only the first.

region.description: Optional coarse region hint for portal routing (e.g. a state/Bundesland or county), derived by the matcher.

queryHints.description: Optional pre-built query strings (matcher's QueryGenerator). A finder MAY use them, but SHOULD prefer building portal-optimal queries from the structured fields above.

NameEntry.description: A single searchable name. At least one of full / surname / given must be present.

kind.description: Provenance of the name within the tree.

full.description: Full display name, plain text (no GEDCOM slashes, no markup).

BirthSpec.description: Birth date, resilient to imprecise GEDCOM dates (ABT/BET..AND/AFT/BEF). Supply exactDate when known to the day; ALWAYS supply yearRange as the resolved interval the true year must fall into (min == max for an exact year). This lets a finder filter by a year window without parsing GEDCOM. The structured fields are authoritative — the original GEDCOM string is intentionally NOT transmitted (PII minimisation). Producer obligation: yearRange.min <= yearRange.max (cannot be expressed in JSON Schema); a consumer MAY reject or swap a reversed range.

yearRange.description: Inclusive [min, max] year interval covering the GEDCOM date's uncertainty.

kind.description: Role of this place for the person. ADVISORY: a finder MAY use kind for routing/weighting but MUST NOT require it; ignoring kind and matching on value is a valid implementation.

value.description: Full GEDCOM place, possibly comma-separated from most-specific to least (e.g. "Musterhausen, Musterstadt, Musterland").

priority.description: 1 = highest. Absent priority is treated as 1 (most important). Lower priority hints may be skipped under rate limits.

dedupKey.description: Stable key so the finder runs an identical phrase only once per job.
